title: No, China did not manage to avoid a crash
gist: Smith argues China did in fact suffer a recession from its 2021+ property bust despite official statistics smoothed to never show contraction, that its 'financial policy' of directing captive banks to shift lending from real estate to manufacturing merely masked and postponed the damage, and that independent estimates show 2022 GDP actually shrank while long-run productivity costs loom.

themes.theme: China's third macro tool: financial policy

insights.claim: China wields a distinctive third stabilization tool beyond monetary and fiscal policy — 'financial policy,' using direct control over its banking system to order banks to lend, rather than merely cutting rates and hoping credit flows.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A genuine institutional innovation worth studying, but it substitutes administrative credit allocation for market pricing, embedding misallocation risk.
insights.quote: China uses both of those, but it also uses a third policy: financial policy.

insights.claim: After the 2021 property crash China ran the same 2009/2015 playbook in reverse, ordering its captive banks to surge industrial/manufacturing loans as real estate loans petered out, redirecting the credit engine rather than deleveraging.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Explains the manufacturing/export overcapacity surge as a stabilization side-effect; the credit didn't shrink, it just changed destination.
insights.quote: industrial loans surged, even as real estate loans petered out
insights.locator: In fact, China did this by resorting to a version

themes.theme: The property bust and its scale

insights.claim: China's 2010s property lending fueled the biggest property boom the world has ever seen, which ended in late 2021 when Evergrande's collapse triggered a sector-wide cascade of bankruptcies and continuously falling prices and housing construction.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The property drag is ongoing (prices still falling), constraining household wealth and consumption for years.
insights.quote: The crash of the Chinese property developer Evergrande began a sequence of bankruptcies and defaults across the entire real estate sector.

insights.claim: Even by China's own official statistics the economy shrank 0.8% quarter-on-quarter in Q2 2022 — an annualized contraction of over -3%, revised up from an initially reported -9.3% — contradicting claims that China avoided any quarter of contraction.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The 'no crash' narrative is false even on Beijing's own numbers; use QoQ not YoY framing to see the contraction.
insights.quote: the Chinese economy shrank by 0.8% in the second quarter of 2022

insights.claim: China smooths its growth numbers — fudging down in good years and up in bad years — with Nakamura et al. (2016) finding official data understated growth in the late 1990s but overstated it since 2002, with real growth dipping negative in 2007-2008.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Treat Chinese official GDP as a smoothed political signal, not a measurement; the smoothing only holds if the economy eventually bounces back to repay 'borrowed' growth.
insights.quote: Our estimates suggest that official statistics present a smoothed version of reality.
insights.locator: There is evidence that the Chinese government

insights.claim: The Rhodium Group estimates China's real GDP actually contracted -0.3% to -0.8% in 2022 and grew only 1.5-2% in 2023, versus official prints of 3.0% and 5.2%, given collapsing property investment and weak household consumption.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Independent estimates put a multi-point wedge below headline growth; size China exposure to the lower trajectory, not the official one.
insights.quote: We estimate that real GDP growth was closer to a contraction of -0.3 percent to -0.8 percent in 2022, and there was only modest growth of 1.5 percent to 2 percent in 2023.
insights.locator: A bunch of analyses claim that China has also done this

insights.claim: China's youth unemployment rose enough in 2023 that the government switched to a narrower definition, yet the upward trend persisted, and alternative labor indicators (non-manufacturing PMI employment, flat migrant worker numbers) told a far more pessimistic story than official headline unemployment.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Labor-market weakness confirms the crash; rely on alternative indicators since headline unemployment is insensitive to migrant and discouraged workers.
insights.locator: The first way to see this is to look at China's job market

insights.claim: China has slipped into deflation since the real estate bust — a classic sign of low aggregate demand and a slowing economy — reinforcing that the downturn is demand-driven and real.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Persistent Chinese deflation is a demand-weakness signal with global spillovers via cheap exports; watch CPI as a real-time gauge of the downturn.
insights.quote: Deflation is a classic sign of low aggregate demand and a slowing economy.
insights.locator: One particularly pessimistic indicator is inflation

themes.theme: Long-run costs and the limits of the model

insights.claim: China's repeated use of stabilization lending directs resources to inefficient companies and lowers productivity growth, raising the risk that the 2022-2024 financial stimulus leaves an overhang of unproductive 'zombie' firms soaking up labor and capital for years.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The stabilization tool carries a deferred productivity tax; long-run Chinese growth potential may be structurally impaired.

insights.claim: China has not rewritten the rules of aggregate demand and supply; if it is now on a permanently lower growth trajectory, there will be too few good years to repay the growth 'borrowed' by smoothing during the bad years of 2022 onward.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The bull case that China 'beat the cycle' is unfounded; the smoothing accounting only works if a strong rebound materializes, which remains unproven.
insights.quote: China still hasn't managed to rewrite the rules of aggregate demand and aggregate supply.
insights.locator: But at the same time, I don't think we ought to be idolizing
